explanation_md: `*` has higher precedence than `+`, so `y * 2` is evaluated first: `4 * 2 = 8`. Then `x + 8 = 3 + 8 = 11`. If you read left-to-right ignoring precedence you would get `(3 + 4) * 2 = 14`, which is wrong.

prompt_md: Which of the following best describes what an *expression* is in C++?

options.a: Any combination of literals, variables, and operators that evaluates to a single value.
options.b: A complete instruction terminated by a semicolon that the program executes.
options.c: Only arithmetic formulas involving the `+` and `*` operators.
options.d: A line of code that declares a new variable.

explanation_md: An expression is any construct that evaluates to a value — it can be a single literal, a variable, or operators applied to sub-expressions. Option b describes an expression *statement*. Option c is too narrow; even a lone variable name is a valid expression.
`a` starts at `5`. After `a = a + 3`, `a` is `8`. After `a = a * 2`, `a` is `16`. Each assignment evaluates the right side using the current value of `a`, then overwrites `a`.

options.b: `5` — the expression `score + 10` computes a value but does not modify `score`.
options.c: `10` — the standalone expression replaces the old value with `10`.
options.d: A compile error because `score + 10;` is not a valid statement.

explanation_md: The expression `score + 10` produces the value `15` but the result is immediately discarded; `score` is never modified. To update `score`, the student needs `score = score + 10;`. The standalone expression is valid C++ (just useless), so there is no compile error.
